snapshot.title: Extract api.py — command API layer between store and CLI
Demote BeanStore.claim/release/close to pure CRUD
Move find_beans_dir and project constants out of cli.py
Delete dead graph.py or wire it into store
Make CLI commands thin: parse → api call → format
Extract api.py — command API layer between store and CLI
snapshot.body:
Epic: Introduce an api.py module between store.py and cli.py to separate three responsibilities:
- store.py: pure SQL CRUD (insert, select, update, delete)
- api.py: command API — each function is one use case, composes store calls, returns models
- cli.py: thin wiring — parse args, call api, format output

Currently cli.py mixes coordination with output (e.g. close does update+get), and store.py mixes CRUD with coordination (claim, release). The goal is every CLI command calls exactly one api function.

snapshot.title: Demote BeanStore.claim/release/close to pure CRUD
snapshot.body:
BeanStore currently has high-level methods (claim, release, release_mine) that compose multiple CRUD operations — get+check+update+get. These belong in the api layer, not the store.

Steps:
- Remove claim(), release(), release_mine() from BeanStore
- BeanStore should only have: create, get, update, delete, list, ready
- Move the coordination logic to api.py (created in sibling task)
- Update tests: store tests verify pure CRUD, api tests verify coordination
- Ensure BeanStore.update() returns the updated Bean (not just rowcount) so callers don't need a separate get()

snapshot.title: Move find_beans_dir and project constants out of cli.py
snapshot.body:
cli.py contains find_beans_dir(), BEANS_DIR, DB_NAME, AGENTS_MD and init logic. These are project discovery concerns, not CLI concerns.

Steps:
- Create project.py with: BEANS_DIR, DB_NAME, AGENTS_MD constants, find_beans_dir(), init_project()
- cli.py imports from project.py and stays as thin wiring
- Update tests/test_init.py to import from project.py
- find_beans_dir is a pure function (no I/O beyond Path.is_dir), fits project.py

snapshot.title: Delete dead graph.py or wire it into store
snapshot.body:
graph.py has pure functions (blocked_ids, blocked_by_children, ready) that duplicate the SQL CTE in BeanStore.ready(). Nobody imports graph.py — it's dead code from Phase 3.3 superseded by the Phase 3.4 SQL implementation.

Decision needed: delete graph.py entirely (SQL CTE is the source of truth) or keep graph.py and have BeanStore.ready() delegate to it. Deleting is simpler — the SQL CTE is tested and performant. If deleted, also remove tests/test_graph.py.

snapshot.title: Make CLI commands thin: parse → api call → format
snapshot.body:
After api.py exists and store is pure CRUD, rewrite each CLI command to follow the pattern:
  parse args → call one api function → format output

Currently broken commands:
- create: constructs Bean model itself (should call api.create(title=, parent_id=))
- close: calls store.update + store.get (should call api.close(bean_id))
- update: validates + store.update + store.get (should call api.update(bean_id, **fields))

Already clean (just need import change): show, delete, list, ready, claim, release.
Also: error handling should stay in CLI (try/except + error() helper).

snapshot.title: Add --reason flag to close command
Add --reason flag to close command
snapshot.body:
When closing a bean, allow recording why it was closed via --reason. This supports workflows where the close reason captures a commit SHA, a decision, or a cancellation explanation.

Implementation options:

1. New close_reason field on Bean model
   Add close_reason: str | None = None to Bean. Store it in the beans table. The close command accepts --reason and persists it alongside status=closed and closed_at.
   Pro: first-class, queryable, shows up in show/list/export.
   Con: adds a column that is null for most beans.

2. Append to body on close
   close command appends a Closed section to the body with the reason text.
   Pro: no schema change, no new field. Body already exists.
   Con: mixes structured data into freeform text. Hard to query.

3. Use update --body before close
   No code change needed. Workflow convention: update body, then close.
   Pro: zero implementation cost.
   Con: two commands instead of one. Easy to forget.

Recommendation: Option 1. A close_reason field is small, explicit, and aligns with what beads_rust does. It also enables future features like listing closed beans with reasons. The schema change is a single ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN plus updating the journal triggers and SCHEMA string.

snapshot.title: Add --body flag to create command
Add --body flag to create command
snapshot.body:
beans create currently only accepts a title positional arg. The AGENTS.md workflow requires self-contained beans with full bodies, but you have to create then update --body separately. Add --body as an optional flag on create so beans can be fully described in one command.

Files: src/beans/cli.py (add --body param to create command), tests/test_cli.py (test create with --body).
The Bean model already has a body field. This is just CLI wiring.

snapshot.title: Add --parent flag to update command
Add --parent flag to update command
snapshot.body:
update command exposes --title, --status, --priority, --body but not --parent. The store already supports updating parent_id (it is in UPDATABLE_FIELDS). Just need to add the CLI option.

Files: src/beans/cli.py (add --parent param to update), tests/test_cli.py (test update --parent).
Mirror the pattern from create: Annotated[str | None, typer.Option(help='Parent bean id', parser=BeanId)] = None

snapshot.title: Refactor journal triggers to avoid hardcoded Bean fields
Replace fields_validate with a BeanUpdate model
Refactor journal triggers to avoid hardcoded Bean fields
snapshot.body: The 3 SQLite triggers (insert/update/delete) in store.py hardcode all Bean field names in json_object() calls. Adding a field to Bean requires manually updating 3 triggers, risking schema drift. Options: (1) remove triggers, journal in application code using Pydantic serialization, (2) keep triggers but add a test that verifies trigger columns match Bean model fields, (3) use a simpler trigger that captures the whole row. Recommend option 1 since it aligns with the api.py extraction — journaling becomes an api concern, not a trigger.

snapshot.title: Replace fields_validate with a BeanUpdate model
snapshot.body: Bean.fields_validate() constructs a dummy Bean instance to validate partial updates. This is hacky — it relies on fake id/title values. Replace with a BeanUpdate Pydantic model with all-optional fields that handles partial validation idiomatically. Used in cli.py update command.

snapshot.title: [design] BaseStore inheritance adds complexity without benefit
[design] BaseStore inheritance adds complexity without benefit
snapshot.body:
**Area:** store.py — BaseStore, BeanStore, DepStore, JournalStore, Store

**Concern:** BaseStore exists solely to share `__init__(self, conn)` and `from_path` across BeanStore, DepStore, JournalStore, and Store. But these substores are never constructed independently — they're always created by Store.__init__ which passes them a connection. The BaseStore.from_path() classmethod is only meaningful on Store itself, yet it's inherited by BeanStore/DepStore/JournalStore where it would produce a broken object (no schema init). The inheritance hierarchy (BaseStore → BeanStore/DepStore/JournalStore, BaseStore → Store) adds a layer of indirection for a single shared line (`self.conn = conn`).

**Alternative:** Remove BaseStore. BeanStore/DepStore/JournalStore each take `conn` in their __init__ directly (one line). Store owns from_path() and __enter__/__exit__. This follows the project's 'functions over methods' and 'no over-engineering' principles — don't build abstractions until you have two concrete uses, and the substores never use from_path independently.

**Tradeoff:** Repeating `self.conn = conn` in 3 classes (trivial) vs. eliminating a misleading inheritance chain. Net simplification.

snapshot.title: [design] Module-level mutable state dict in cli.py
[design] Module-level mutable state dict in cli.py
snapshot.body:
**Area:** cli.py — `state: dict = {}` at module level

**Concern:** cli.py uses a module-level mutable dict `state = {}` to pass --db, --json, and --dry-run from the Typer callback to commands. This is hidden global state that violates the project's 'pure by default' and 'default arguments for configurability' principles. It makes the data flow invisible — you have to know that main() populates state and get_store()/output()/error() read from it. It also makes the output() function impure despite looking like a pure formatter, since error() reads state['json'] from the global. In tests this works because CliRunner serializes invocations, but it's fragile.

**Alternative:** Use Typer's Context object (`ctx.obj`) to thread options through. Or, since the existing beans already plan to extract api.py, the CLI wiring could construct a config namedtuple in the callback and pass it to api functions. Either way, the dependency becomes explicit.

**Tradeoff:** Slightly more boilerplate in each command signature vs. eliminating hidden coupling. This is a moderate concern — the current approach works but fights the project's own design values.

snapshot.title: [design] DryRunConnection uses __getattr__ proxy — fragile and implicit
[design] DryRunConnection uses __getattr__ proxy — fragile and implicit
snapshot.body:
**Area:** store.py — DryRunConnection class

**Concern:** DryRunConnection wraps a sqlite3.Connection via __getattr__ delegation, suppresses context-manager commits by making __exit__ a no-op, and manually controls isolation_level. This is clever but fragile: (1) it relies on the substores using `with self.conn:` for writes, which is an undocumented protocol, (2) __getattr__ proxy means type checkers can't verify the interface, (3) it mixes two separate concerns — transaction control and connection lifecycle, (4) the JournalStore deliberately gets the unwrapped conn (line 322) to avoid the dry-run wrapper, which is a subtle correctness detail easy to break.

**Alternative:** A simpler approach: Store could begin a transaction at init when dry_run=True, and rollback in close(). The substores don't need to know about dry-run at all — they already use `with self.conn:` which creates savepoints inside an active transaction. No proxy class needed, just two lines in Store.__init__ and Store.close(). This also eliminates the split where JournalStore gets raw conn while others get wrapped conn.

**Tradeoff:** Removes a class and ~20 lines of proxy machinery in exchange for 2-3 lines of transaction control in Store. Simpler mental model.

snapshot.title: [design] ready() query returns closed beans
[test] Missing fan-in dependency test for ready()
[design] ready() query returns closed beans
snapshot.body:
**Area:** store.py — BeanStore.ready() SQL query

**Concern:** The ready() query returns all non-blocked beans including closed ones. This is semantically odd — 'ready' implies 'available to work on', but closed beans are finished. The test_closed_blocker_does_not_block test (line 156) explicitly asserts closed beans appear in ready results. The CLI `beans ready` output showing closed beans alongside actionable work adds noise for the user. The graph.py pure implementation has the same behavior (test_closed_beans_still_returned).

**Alternative:** Add `AND status != 'closed'` to the ready() WHERE clause. Closed beans aren't ready for work. If someone needs to see all beans, they use `beans list`.

**Tradeoff:** Minor behavioral change. Might affect agents that rely on seeing closed beans in ready output (unlikely — agents should use `list` for that). Cleaner semantics for the primary use case.

snapshot.title: [design] output() mixes formatting with dispatch via pattern matching
[test] Missing fan-in dependency test for ready()
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_store.py (TestBeanStoreReady) and tests/test_graph.py (TestReady)
**Gap:** No test where a bean is blocked by multiple open blockers (fan-in), and only one blocker is closed. Need to verify the bean stays blocked until ALL blockers are closed.
**Risk:** A regression in the CTE could unblock a bean when only one of its multiple blockers is closed, allowing premature work.
**Suggestion:** Add test_fan_in_partial_close: create A, B, C. Add deps A->C and B->C. Close A. Assert C is NOT in ready. Close B. Assert C IS in ready.

snapshot.title: [test] Missing test for delete bean with existing deps (FK constraint)
[design] output() mixes formatting with dispatch via pattern matching
snapshot.body:
**Area:** cli.py — output() function

**Concern:** output() uses structural pattern matching on (data, json) tuples to dispatch across Bean/Dep/list × json/text combinations. This is clever but has issues: (1) it returns a string but callers immediately typer.echo() it — why not echo directly? (2) the list branch recurses into output() for text mode but calls model_dump_json() for JSON mode — inconsistent paths, (3) the function shadows the built-in `json` name, (4) the empty string fallback (line 54) silently swallows unknown types. The function tries to be a universal formatter but would be clearer as separate format_bean/format_dep/format_list functions that each handle their json flag.

**Alternative:** This is somewhat covered by bean-f58060fb (make CLI thin) which will restructure output handling. But even after that refactor, the output() function's design should be reconsidered. Simpler: each command formats its own output inline (it's 1-2 lines each), or have format_bean(bean, json) and format_list(items, json) as focused helpers.

**Tradeoff:** More functions but each one obvious. Low priority since the api.py extraction will force this to be rethought anyway.

snapshot.title: [test] Missing test for delete bean with existing deps (FK constraint)
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_store.py (TestBeanStoreDeleteBean)
**Gap:** delete is only tested on beans without dependencies. Deleting a bean that is a from_id or to_id in the deps table raises sqlite3.IntegrityError (FOREIGN KEY constraint failed), but no test documents this behavior.
**Risk:** This is a critical path — a user trying to delete a blocker bean gets an unhandled sqlite3 error instead of a clear message. The CLI has no try/except for IntegrityError in the delete command, so it would crash. Either the behavior should cascade-delete deps, or it should produce a user-friendly error. Either way, the current behavior is untested.
**Suggestion:** Add test_delete_bean_with_deps: create A and B, add dep A->B, attempt delete(A), assert it raises IntegrityError (or whatever the intended behavior is). Also add a CLI test for the same scenario.

snapshot.title: [design] replay() disables journal triggers causing silent schema divergence
[test] Missing test for ready() ordering by priority
[design] replay() disables journal triggers causing silent schema divergence
snapshot.body:
**Area:** store.py — JournalStore.replay()

**Concern:** replay() re-executes raw INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE SQL against the beans table, which fires the journal triggers again — meaning replaying N journal entries produces N new journal entries in the target database. This is a design bug: after rebuild, the target db has a journal that's a replay artifact, not the original history. The replay is not idempotent.

Additionally, replay() uses INSERT_BEAN and UPDATE_BEAN_ALL constants which duplicate the column ordering from bean_values(). If a field is added to Bean but not to these constants, replay silently corrupts data.

**Alternative:** replay() should either (1) temporarily disable triggers during replay then copy the original journal entries verbatim, or (2) replay should be a journal-level operation that inserts journal rows directly and derives the current state. Option 1 is simpler and preserves history accurately.

**Tradeoff:** Slightly more complex replay logic vs. correct journal preservation. This is a real correctness issue, not just aesthetics.

snapshot.title: [test] Missing test for ready() ordering by priority
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_store.py (TestBeanStoreReady)
**Gap:** The ready() query has no ORDER BY clause. The AGENTS.md workflow says 'pick highest priority unblocked bean from beans ready' — but ready() returns beans in arbitrary SQLite row order, not sorted by priority. No test asserts ordering.
**Risk:** Agents relying on ready() to return priority-sorted beans will pick the wrong task. If an ORDER BY is added later, there's no test to prevent regression.
**Suggestion:** Add test_ready_ordered_by_priority: create beans with priority 0, 2, 1. Assert ready() returns them in priority order [0, 1, 2]. If ordering is not intended, this documents the design decision.

snapshot.title: [design] Bean.type is unvalidated free-text string
[test] Missing test for claim on already-closed bean
[test] Missing test for claim on already-closed bean
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_store.py (TestBeanStoreClaim)
**Gap:** claim() is tested for unclaimed, same-actor (idempotent), and different-actor cases. But there's no test for claiming a bean that is already closed. The current implementation would happily set status='in_progress' on a closed bean, effectively reopening it as a side effect.
**Risk:** A user could accidentally reopen a closed bean by claiming it. This is likely undesired — claiming a closed bean should either be a no-op or raise an error.
**Suggestion:** Add test_claim_closed_bean: create a bean, close it, attempt claim. Assert the expected behavior (either error or the bean stays closed).

snapshot.title: [design] Bean.type is unvalidated free-text string
snapshot.body:
**Area:** models.py — Bean.type field

**Concern:** Bean.status uses Literal['open', 'in_progress', 'closed'] for validation, but Bean.type is a plain `str` with default 'task'. The AGENTS.md documentation references specific types (task, bug, epic) that are meaningful for workflow. Without validation, typos like 'taks' or 'epci' are silently accepted. This is an inconsistency — status is constrained but type is not, even though both are categorical fields.

**Alternative:** Use Literal['task', 'bug', 'epic'] for type, matching how status is handled. If extensibility is desired, at minimum document why type is intentionally unconstrained.

**Tradeoff:** Constraining type loses flexibility for custom types, but gains consistency and typo protection. If custom types are valuable, keep it open but acknowledge the design choice.

snapshot.title: [test] Missing CLI test for --body update
[test] Missing CLI test for --body update
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_cli.py (TestUpdateCommand)
**Gap:** The update CLI command accepts --title, --status, --priority, and --body options. Tests cover --title, --status, and --priority but not --body. The body field is the primary place for detailed task descriptions.
**Risk:** Low — the code path is straightforward (same as other fields). But body content often contains special characters, newlines, and multi-line text that could trip up serialization.
**Suggestion:** Add test_update_body: create a bean, update with --body 'Detailed description', verify the body is set correctly in JSON output.

snapshot.title: [test] Journal replay does not restore deps
[test] Journal replay does not restore deps
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_journal.py (TestJournalReplay)
**Gap:** Journal triggers only capture beans table changes (create/update/delete). Deps table changes are NOT journaled — there are no triggers on the deps table. This means export-journal + rebuild loses all dependency information. No test verifies this gap.
**Risk:** Critical data loss. A user who rebuilds from journal will lose all dependency relationships. The ready() command will return wrong results on the rebuilt database. This is especially dangerous because the rebuild command exists and appears to promise full database reconstruction.
**Suggestion:** Add test_replay_does_not_restore_deps: create two beans, add dep, export journal, replay into new db, verify deps are missing. This documents the known limitation. Separately, consider adding journal triggers for the deps table.

snapshot.title: [integration] --dry-run rebuild commits data through raw conn bypass
[test] BeanId runtime validation doesn't match JSON schema
[semantic] ready() wrongly propagates blocking through closed intermediate beans
[test] BeanId runtime validation doesn't match JSON schema
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_models.py (TestBeanValidation) and tests/test_store.py (TestBeanStoreValidation)
**Gap:** BeanId.__get_pydantic_json_schema__ advertises the pattern ^bean-[0-9a-f]{8}$ but BeanId.__new__ only checks startswith('bean-'). So BeanId('bean-ZZZZ') or BeanId('bean-toolong1234') pass runtime validation but violate the published schema. No test catches this mismatch.
**Risk:** Agents consuming the schema will expect strict hex IDs, but the system accepts anything after 'bean-'. If a malformed ID is stored, downstream consumers trusting the schema contract will fail.
**Suggestion:** Add test_bean_id_rejects_non_hex: assert BeanId('bean-ZZZZZZZZ') raises ValueError. If the loose validation is intentional, add test_bean_id_accepts_any_suffix and update the JSON schema to match.

snapshot.title: [integration] --dry-run rebuild commits data through raw conn bypass
snapshot.body:
**Changed:** src/beans/store.py:Store.__init__ (line 322) gives JournalStore the raw conn, not the DryRunConnection wrapper.
**Affected:** src/beans/store.py:JournalStore.replay (line 276) — uses 'with self.conn:' which commits on the raw conn.
**Risk:** Running 'beans --dry-run rebuild journal.jsonl' permanently commits all replayed data. DryRunConnection sets isolation_level=None and opens a BEGIN on the shared conn object, but JournalStore's 'with self.conn:' calls __exit__ on the raw sqlite3.Connection, which COMMITs the open transaction. After that, Store.close()'s rollback() is a no-op because there's nothing to roll back.
**Verified:** Experimentally confirmed — data persists after rollback when JournalStore uses raw conn in dry-run mode.
**Fix:** Either (a) pass the wrapped DryRunConnection to JournalStore too, or (b) have JournalStore.replay() skip the 'with self.conn:' context manager and rely on the outer transaction management.

snapshot.title: [integration] templates/AGENTS.md references --body on create which does not exist
[semantic] ready() wrongly propagates blocking through closed intermediate beans
snapshot.body:
**Files:** src/beans/store.py:167-180 (BeanStore.ready SQL) and src/beans/graph.py:6-17 (blocked_ids)

**Issue:** Both ready() implementations propagate blocking transitively through closed intermediate beans. If A(open) blocks B(closed) and B blocks C(open), C is wrongly reported as blocked even though its only direct blocker (B) is closed and done.

**Root cause — SQL (store.py):** The recursive CTE `blocked_by_deps` first finds beans blocked by open blockers (correct), then extends transitively: any bean in `blocked_by_deps` propagates blocking to its dependents. But a closed bean that happens to be in `blocked_by_deps` should not propagate — it is done.

**Root cause — Python (graph.py):** Same logic: `blocked_ids()` adds `dep.to_id` to blocked if `dep.from_id in blocked`, without checking whether `from_id` is closed.

**Expected:** A closed bean should never block anything, even if it is itself blocked by an open bean. The transitive step should only propagate through open beans.

**Repro:**
```python
A(open) -> B(closed) -> C(open)
ready() returns [A]         # actual
ready() should return [A, B, C]  # expected (B is closed, C is unblocked)
```

**Fix:** In the recursive CTE, join with `beans` and add `WHERE b.status != 'closed'` for the from_id. In graph.py, check `dep.from_id in open_ids` (not `in blocked`) during transitive propagation, or skip closed from_ids.

snapshot.title: [integration] templates/AGENTS.md references --body on create which does not exist
snapshot.body:
**Changed:** src/beans/cli.py:create (line 113) only accepts title and --parent. There is no --body flag.
**Affected:** src/beans/templates/AGENTS.md lines 22, 36, 62 — all reference 'beans create ... --body ...'. Also AGENTS.md (project root) lines 290, 294 reference the same.
**Risk:** Agents following the template instructions will call 'beans create "Title" --body "desc"' which silently fails or errors. The --body flag only exists on the update command. This causes incorrect agent workflows — beans get created without bodies, requiring a follow-up update.
**Fix:** Either (a) add --body to the create command in cli.py, or (b) update templates/AGENTS.md and AGENTS.md to use a two-step workflow: create then update --body.

snapshot.title: [design] output() mixes formatting with dispatch via pattern matching
snapshot.body: Duplicate of bean-f58060fb (Make CLI commands thin). Output formatting will be addressed as part of CLI thinning.

snapshot.title: [integration] templates/AGENTS.md references --body on create which does not exist
snapshot.body: Duplicate of bean-69b4e720 (Add --body flag to create). Template will be correct once --body is implemented.

snapshot.title: [design] replay() disables journal triggers causing silent schema divergence
snapshot.body: Closely related to bean-4095cfba (Refactor journal triggers). Moving journaling to application code (option 1 in that bean) will fix this too.

snapshot.title: Add --body flag to create command
snapshot.body:
beans create currently only accepts a title positional arg. The AGENTS.md workflow requires self-contained beans with full bodies, but you have to create then update --body separately. Add --body as an optional flag on create so beans can be fully described in one command.

Files: src/beans/cli.py (add --body param to create command), tests/test_cli.py (test create with --body).
The Bean model already has a body field. This is just CLI wiring.

The Bean model already has a body field. This is just CLI wiring.

update command exposes --title, --status, --priority, --body but not --parent. The store already supports updating parent_id (it is in UPDATABLE_FIELDS). Just need to add the CLI option.

snapshot.title: Add --parent flag to update command
snapshot.body:
update command exposes --title, --status, --priority, --body but not --parent. The store already supports updating parent_id (it is in UPDATABLE_FIELDS). Just need to add the CLI option.

snapshot.title: [semantic] ready() wrongly propagates blocking through closed intermediate beans
snapshot.body:
**Files:** src/beans/store.py:167-180 (BeanStore.ready SQL) and src/beans/graph.py:6-17 (blocked_ids)

**Issue:** Both ready() implementations propagate blocking transitively through closed intermediate beans. If A(open) blocks B(closed) and B blocks C(open), C is wrongly reported as blocked even though its only direct blocker (B) is closed and done.

**Root cause — SQL (store.py):** The recursive CTE `blocked_by_deps` first finds beans blocked by open blockers (correct), then extends transitively: any bean in `blocked_by_deps` propagates blocking to its dependents. But a closed bean that happens to be in `blocked_by_deps` should not propagate — it is done.

**Root cause — Python (graph.py):** Same logic: `blocked_ids()` adds `dep.to_id` to blocked if `dep.from_id in blocked`, without checking whether `from_id` is closed.

**Expected:** A closed bean should never block anything, even if it is itself blocked by an open bean. The transitive step should only propagate through open beans.

**Repro:**
```python
A(open) -> B(closed) -> C(open)
ready() returns [A]         # actual
ready() should return [A, B, C]  # expected (B is closed, C is unblocked)
```

**Fix:** In the recursive CTE, join with `beans` and add `WHERE b.status != 'closed'` for the from_id. In graph.py, check `dep.from_id in open_ids` (not `in blocked`) during transitive propagation, or skip closed from_ids.

snapshot.title: [semantic] ready() wrongly propagates blocking through closed intermediate beans
snapshot.body:
**Files:** src/beans/store.py:167-180 (BeanStore.ready SQL) and src/beans/graph.py:6-17 (blocked_ids)

**Issue:** Both ready() implementations propagate blocking transitively through closed intermediate beans. If A(open) blocks B(closed) and B blocks C(open), C is wrongly reported as blocked even though its only direct blocker (B) is closed and done.

**Root cause — SQL (store.py):** The recursive CTE `blocked_by_deps` first finds beans blocked by open blockers (correct), then extends transitively: any bean in `blocked_by_deps` propagates blocking to its dependents. But a closed bean that happens to be in `blocked_by_deps` should not propagate — it is done.

**Root cause — Python (graph.py):** Same logic: `blocked_ids()` adds `dep.to_id` to blocked if `dep.from_id in blocked`, without checking whether `from_id` is closed.

**Expected:** A closed bean should never block anything, even if it is itself blocked by an open bean. The transitive step should only propagate through open beans.

**Repro:**
```python
A(open) -> B(closed) -> C(open)
ready() returns [A]         # actual
ready() should return [A, B, C]  # expected (B is closed, C is unblocked)
```

**Fix:** In the recursive CTE, join with `beans` and add `WHERE b.status != 'closed'` for the from_id. In graph.py, check `dep.from_id in open_ids` (not `in blocked`) during transitive propagation, or skip closed from_ids.

snapshot.title: [test] Missing test for delete bean with existing deps (FK constraint)
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_store.py (TestBeanStoreDeleteBean)
**Gap:** delete is only tested on beans without dependencies. Deleting a bean that is a from_id or to_id in the deps table raises sqlite3.IntegrityError (FOREIGN KEY constraint failed), but no test documents this behavior.
**Risk:** This is a critical path — a user trying to delete a blocker bean gets an unhandled sqlite3 error instead of a clear message. The CLI has no try/except for IntegrityError in the delete command, so it would crash. Either the behavior should cascade-delete deps, or it should produce a user-friendly error. Either way, the current behavior is untested.
**Suggestion:** Add test_delete_bean_with_deps: create A and B, add dep A->B, attempt delete(A), assert it raises IntegrityError (or whatever the intended behavior is). Also add a CLI test for the same scenario.

snapshot.title: [integration] --dry-run rebuild commits data through raw conn bypass
snapshot.body:
**Changed:** src/beans/store.py:Store.__init__ (line 322) gives JournalStore the raw conn, not the DryRunConnection wrapper.
**Affected:** src/beans/store.py:JournalStore.replay (line 276) — uses 'with self.conn:' which commits on the raw conn.
**Risk:** Running 'beans --dry-run rebuild journal.jsonl' permanently commits all replayed data. DryRunConnection sets isolation_level=None and opens a BEGIN on the shared conn object, but JournalStore's 'with self.conn:' calls __exit__ on the raw sqlite3.Connection, which COMMITs the open transaction. After that, Store.close()'s rollback() is a no-op because there's nothing to roll back.
**Verified:** Experimentally confirmed — data persists after rollback when JournalStore uses raw conn in dry-run mode.
**Fix:** Either (a) pass the wrapped DryRunConnection to JournalStore too, or (b) have JournalStore.replay() skip the 'with self.conn:' context manager and rely on the outer transaction management.

After that, Store.close()'s rollback() is a no-op because there's nothing to roll back.

snapshot.title: [test] Journal replay does not restore deps
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_journal.py (TestJournalReplay)
**Gap:** Journal triggers only capture beans table changes (create/update/delete). Deps table changes are NOT journaled — there are no triggers on the deps table. This means export-journal + rebuild loses all dependency information. No test verifies this gap.
**Risk:** Critical data loss. A user who rebuilds from journal will lose all dependency relationships. The ready() command will return wrong results on the rebuilt database. This is especially dangerous because the rebuild command exists and appears to promise full database reconstruction.
**Suggestion:** Add test_replay_does_not_restore_deps: create two beans, add dep, export journal, replay into new db, verify deps are missing. This documents the known limitation. Separately, consider adding journal triggers for the deps table.

snapshot.title: Delete dead graph.py or wire it into store
snapshot.body:
graph.py has pure functions (blocked_ids, blocked_by_children, ready) that duplicate the SQL CTE in BeanStore.ready(). Nobody imports graph.py — it's dead code from Phase 3.3 superseded by the Phase 3.4 SQL implementation.

Decision needed: delete graph.py entirely (SQL CTE is the source of truth) or keep graph.py and have BeanStore.ready() delegate to it. Deleting is simpler — the SQL CTE is tested and performant. If deleted, also remove tests/test_graph.py.

snapshot.title: [test] Missing fan-in dependency test for ready()
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_store.py (TestBeanStoreReady) and tests/test_graph.py (TestReady)
**Gap:** No test where a bean is blocked by multiple open blockers (fan-in), and only one blocker is closed. Need to verify the bean stays blocked until ALL blockers are closed.
**Risk:** A regression in the CTE could unblock a bean when only one of its multiple blockers is closed, allowing premature work.
**Suggestion:** Add test_fan_in_partial_close: create A, B, C. Add deps A->C and B->C. Close A. Assert C is NOT in ready. Close B. Assert C IS in ready.

snapshot.title: [test] Missing test for ready() ordering by priority
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_store.py (TestBeanStoreReady)
**Gap:** The ready() query has no ORDER BY clause. The AGENTS.md workflow says 'pick highest priority unblocked bean from beans ready' — but ready() returns beans in arbitrary SQLite row order, not sorted by priority. No test asserts ordering.
**Risk:** Agents relying on ready() to return priority-sorted beans will pick the wrong task. If an ORDER BY is added later, there's no test to prevent regression.
**Suggestion:** Add test_ready_ordered_by_priority: create beans with priority 0, 2, 1. Assert ready() returns them in priority order [0, 1, 2]. If ordering is not intended, this documents the design decision.

snapshot.title: [test] Missing test for claim on already-closed bean
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_store.py (TestBeanStoreClaim)
**Gap:** claim() is tested for unclaimed, same-actor (idempotent), and different-actor cases. But there's no test for claiming a bean that is already closed. The current implementation would happily set status='in_progress' on a closed bean, effectively reopening it as a side effect.
**Risk:** A user could accidentally reopen a closed bean by claiming it. This is likely undesired — claiming a closed bean should either be a no-op or raise an error.
**Suggestion:** Add test_claim_closed_bean: create a bean, close it, attempt claim. Assert the expected behavior (either error or the bean stays closed).

snapshot.title: [design] ready() query returns closed beans
snapshot.body:
**Area:** store.py — BeanStore.ready() SQL query

**Concern:** The ready() query returns all non-blocked beans including closed ones. This is semantically odd — 'ready' implies 'available to work on', but closed beans are finished. The test_closed_blocker_does_not_block test (line 156) explicitly asserts closed beans appear in ready results. The CLI `beans ready` output showing closed beans alongside actionable work adds noise for the user. The graph.py pure implementation has the same behavior (test_closed_beans_still_returned).

**Alternative:** Add `AND status != 'closed'` to the ready() WHERE clause. Closed beans aren't ready for work. If someone needs to see all beans, they use `beans list`.

**Tradeoff:** Minor behavioral change. Might affect agents that rely on seeing closed beans in ready output (unlikely — agents should use `list` for that). Cleaner semantics for the primary use case.

snapshot.title: [test] Missing CLI test for --body update
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_cli.py (TestUpdateCommand)
**Gap:** The update CLI command accepts --title, --status, --priority, and --body options. Tests cover --title, --status, and --priority but not --body. The body field is the primary place for detailed task descriptions.
**Risk:** Low — the code path is straightforward (same as other fields). But body content often contains special characters, newlines, and multi-line text that could trip up serialization.
**Suggestion:** Add test_update_body: create a bean, update with --body 'Detailed description', verify the body is set correctly in JSON output.

snapshot.title: [design] Bean.type is unvalidated free-text string
snapshot.body:
**Area:** models.py — Bean.type field

**Concern:** Bean.status uses Literal['open', 'in_progress', 'closed'] for validation, but Bean.type is a plain `str` with default 'task'. The AGENTS.md documentation references specific types (task, bug, epic) that are meaningful for workflow. Without validation, typos like 'taks' or 'epci' are silently accepted. This is an inconsistency — status is constrained but type is not, even though both are categorical fields.

**Alternative:** Use Literal['task', 'bug', 'epic'] for type, matching how status is handled. If extensibility is desired, at minimum document why type is intentionally unconstrained.

**Tradeoff:** Constraining type loses flexibility for custom types, but gains consistency and typo protection. If custom types are valuable, keep it open but acknowledge the design choice.

snapshot.title: [test] BeanId runtime validation doesn't match JSON schema
snapshot.body:
**Test file:** tests/test_models.py (TestBeanValidation) and tests/test_store.py (TestBeanStoreValidation)
**Gap:** BeanId.__get_pydantic_json_schema__ advertises the pattern ^bean-[0-9a-f]{8}$ but BeanId.__new__ only checks startswith('bean-'). So BeanId('bean-ZZZZ') or BeanId('bean-toolong1234') pass runtime validation but violate the published schema. No test catches this mismatch.
**Risk:** Agents consuming the schema will expect strict hex IDs, but the system accepts anything after 'bean-'. If a malformed ID is stored, downstream consumers trusting the schema contract will fail.
**Suggestion:** Add test_bean_id_rejects_non_hex: assert BeanId('bean-ZZZZZZZZ') raises ValueError. If the loose validation is intentional, add test_bean_id_accepts_any_suffix and update the JSON schema to match.

snapshot.title: Move find_beans_dir and project constants out of cli.py
snapshot.body:
cli.py contains find_beans_dir(), BEANS_DIR, DB_NAME, AGENTS_MD and init logic. These are project discovery concerns, not CLI concerns.

Steps:
- Create project.py with: BEANS_DIR, DB_NAME, AGENTS_MD constants, find_beans_dir(), init_project()
- cli.py imports from project.py and stays as thin wiring
- Update tests/test_init.py to import from project.py
- find_beans_dir is a pure function (no I/O beyond Path.is_dir), fits project.py

cli.py contains find_beans_dir(), BEANS_DIR, DB_NAME, AGENTS_MD and init logic. These are project discovery concerns, not CLI concerns.

Steps:

snapshot.title: [design] BaseStore inheritance adds complexity without benefit
snapshot.body:
**Area:** store.py — BaseStore, BeanStore, DepStore, JournalStore, Store

**Concern:** BaseStore exists solely to share `__init__(self, conn)` and `from_path` across BeanStore, DepStore, JournalStore, and Store. But these substores are never constructed independently — they're always created by Store.__init__ which passes them a connection. The BaseStore.from_path() classmethod is only meaningful on Store itself, yet it's inherited by BeanStore/DepStore/JournalStore where it would produce a broken object (no schema init). The inheritance hierarchy (BaseStore → BeanStore/DepStore/JournalStore, BaseStore → Store) adds a layer of indirection for a single shared line (`self.conn = conn`).

**Alternative:** Remove BaseStore. BeanStore/DepStore/JournalStore each take `conn` in their __init__ directly (one line). Store owns from_path() and __enter__/__exit__. This follows the project's 'functions over methods' and 'no over-engineering' principles — don't build abstractions until you have two concrete uses, and the substores never use from_path independently.

**Tradeoff:** Repeating `self.conn = conn` in 3 classes (trivial) vs. eliminating a misleading inheritance chain. Net simplification.

snapshot.title: [design] BaseStore inheritance adds complexity without benefit
snapshot.body:
**Area:** store.py — BaseStore, BeanStore, DepStore, JournalStore, Store

**Concern:** BaseStore exists solely to share `__init__(self, conn)` and `from_path` across BeanStore, DepStore, JournalStore, and Store. But these substores are never constructed independently — they're always created by Store.__init__ which passes them a connection. The BaseStore.from_path() classmethod is only meaningful on Store itself, yet it's inherited by BeanStore/DepStore/JournalStore where it would produce a broken object (no schema init). The inheritance hierarchy (BaseStore → BeanStore/DepStore/JournalStore, BaseStore → Store) adds a layer of indirection for a single shared line (`self.conn = conn`).

**Alternative:** Remove BaseStore. BeanStore/DepStore/JournalStore each take `conn` in their __init__ directly (one line). Store owns from_path() and __enter__/__exit__. This follows the project's 'functions over methods' and 'no over-engineering' principles — don't build abstractions until you have two concrete uses, and the substores never use from_path independently.

**Tradeoff:** Repeating `self.conn = conn` in 3 classes (trivial) vs. eliminating a misleading inheritance chain. Net simplification.

snapshot.title: [design] DryRunConnection uses __getattr__ proxy — fragile and implicit
snapshot.body:
**Area:** store.py — DryRunConnection class

**Concern:** DryRunConnection wraps a sqlite3.Connection via __getattr__ delegation, suppresses context-manager commits by making __exit__ a no-op, and manually controls isolation_level. This is clever but fragile: (1) it relies on the substores using `with self.conn:` for writes, which is an undocumented protocol, (2) __getattr__ proxy means type checkers can't verify the interface, (3) it mixes two separate concerns — transaction control and connection lifecycle, (4) the JournalStore deliberately gets the unwrapped conn (line 322) to avoid the dry-run wrapper, which is a subtle correctness detail easy to break.

**Alternative:** A simpler approach: Store could begin a transaction at init when dry_run=True, and rollback in close(). The substores don't need to know about dry-run at all — they already use `with self.conn:` which creates savepoints inside an active transaction. No proxy class needed, just two lines in Store.__init__ and Store.close(). This also eliminates the split where JournalStore gets raw conn while others get wrapped conn.

**Tradeoff:** Removes a class and ~20 lines of proxy machinery in exchange for 2-3 lines of transaction control in Store. Simpler mental model.

snapshot.title: Replace fields_validate with a BeanUpdate model
snapshot.body: Bean.fields_validate() constructs a dummy Bean instance to validate partial updates. This is hacky — it relies on fake id/title values. Replace with a BeanUpdate Pydantic model with all-optional fields that handles partial validation idiomatically. Used in cli.py update command.

snapshot.title: Replace fields_validate with a BeanUpdate model

snapshot.title: Demote BeanStore.claim/release/close to pure CRUD
snapshot.body:
BeanStore currently has high-level methods (claim, release, release_mine) that compose multiple CRUD operations — get+check+update+get. These belong in the api layer, not the store.

Steps:
- Remove claim(), release(), release_mine() from BeanStore
- BeanStore should only have: create, get, update, delete, list, ready
- Move the coordination logic to api.py (created in sibling task)
- Update tests: store tests verify pure CRUD, api tests verify coordination
- Ensure BeanStore.update() returns the updated Bean (not just rowcount) so callers don't need a separate get()

snapshot.title: Refactor journal triggers to avoid hardcoded Bean fields
snapshot.body: The 3 SQLite triggers (insert/update/delete) in store.py hardcode all Bean field names in json_object() calls. Adding a field to Bean requires manually updating 3 triggers, risking schema drift. Options: (1) remove triggers, journal in application code using Pydantic serialization, (2) keep triggers but add a test that verifies trigger columns match Bean model fields, (3) use a simpler trigger that captures the whole row. Recommend option 1 since it aligns with the api.py extraction — journaling becomes an api concern, not a trigger.

Adding a field to Bean requires manually updating 3 triggers, risking schema drift. Options: (1) remove triggers, journal in application code using Pydantic serialization, (2) keep triggers but add a test that verifies trigger columns match Bean model fields, (3) use a simpler trigger that captures the whole row. Recommend option 1 since it aligns with the api.py extraction — journaling becomes an api concern, not a trigger.

snapshot.title: [design] Module-level mutable state dict in cli.py
snapshot.body:
**Area:** cli.py — `state: dict = {}` at module level

**Concern:** cli.py uses a module-level mutable dict `state = {}` to pass --db, --json, and --dry-run from the Typer callback to commands. This is hidden global state that violates the project's 'pure by default' and 'default arguments for configurability' principles. It makes the data flow invisible — you have to know that main() populates state and get_store()/output()/error() read from it. It also makes the output() function impure despite looking like a pure formatter, since error() reads state['json'] from the global. In tests this works because CliRunner serializes invocations, but it's fragile.

**Alternative:** Use Typer's Context object (`ctx.obj`) to thread options through. Or, since the existing beans already plan to extract api.py, the CLI wiring could construct a config namedtuple in the callback and pass it to api functions. Either way, the dependency becomes explicit.

**Tradeoff:** Slightly more boilerplate in each command signature vs. eliminating hidden coupling. This is a moderate concern — the current approach works but fights the project's own design values.

snapshot.title: [design] Module-level mutable state dict in cli.py
snapshot.body:
**Area:** cli.py — `state: dict = {}` at module level

**Concern:** cli.py uses a module-level mutable dict `state = {}` to pass --db, --json, and --dry-run from the Typer callback to commands. This is hidden global state that violates the project's 'pure by default' and 'default arguments for configurability' principles. It makes the data flow invisible — you have to know that main() populates state and get_store()/output()/error() read from it. It also makes the output() function impure despite looking like a pure formatter, since error() reads state['json'] from the global. In tests this works because CliRunner serializes invocations, but it's fragile.

**Alternative:** Use Typer's Context object (`ctx.obj`) to thread options through. Or, since the existing beans already plan to extract api.py, the CLI wiring could construct a config namedtuple in the callback and pass it to api functions. Either way, the dependency becomes explicit.

**Tradeoff:** Slightly more boilerplate in each command signature vs. eliminating hidden coupling. This is a moderate concern — the current approach works but fights the project's own design values.

snapshot.title: Make CLI commands thin: parse → api call → format
snapshot.body:
After api.py exists and store is pure CRUD, rewrite each CLI command to follow the pattern:
  parse args → call one api function → format output

Currently broken commands:
- create: constructs Bean model itself (should call api.create(title=, parent_id=))
- close: calls store.update + store.get (should call api.close(bean_id))
- update: validates + store.update + store.get (should call api.update(bean_id, **fields))

Already clean (just need import change): show, delete, list, ready, claim, release.
Also: error handling should stay in CLI (try/except + error() helper).

snapshot.title: Make CLI commands thin: parse → api call → format
snapshot.body:
After api.py exists and store is pure CRUD, rewrite each CLI command to follow the pattern:
  parse args → call one api function → format output

Currently broken commands:
- create: constructs Bean model itself (should call api.create(title=, parent_id=))
- close: calls store.update + store.get (should call api.close(bean_id))
- update: validates + store.update + store.get (should call api.update(bean_id, **fields))

Already clean (just need import change): show, delete, list, ready, claim, release.
Also: error handling should stay in CLI (try/except + error() helper).

snapshot.title: Extract api.py — command API layer between store and CLI
snapshot.body:
Epic: Introduce an api.py module between store.py and cli.py to separate three responsibilities:
- store.py: pure SQL CRUD (insert, select, update, delete)
- api.py: command API — each function is one use case, composes store calls, returns models
- cli.py: thin wiring — parse args, call api, format output

Currently cli.py mixes coordination with output (e.g. close does update+get), and store.py mixes CRUD with coordination (claim, release). The goal is every CLI command calls exactly one api function.
snapshot.close_reason: All subtasks completed. api.py extracted in 25aebfc, CLI thinned in 5f93ee8

snapshot.title: Phase 7: Recipes & Config
snapshot.body: Epic for global config, agent recipes, and cross-project dependencies. See PLAN.md Phase 7.

snapshot.title: Global config (~/.config/beans/config.json)
snapshot.body: Add 'beans config' command with XDG path resolution. Config reads from ~/.config/beans/config.json. Allows setting defaults like default priority, actor name, etc.

snapshot.title: Agent integration recipes
snapshot.body: 'beans recipe <client>' command that outputs agent-specific instructions. Bundled recipes for Claude, GPT, etc. Each recipe is a text template with project-specific placeholders.

snapshot.title: Cross-project dependency tracking
snapshot.body: Support deps across projects. projects.json registry maps project names to .beans/ paths. 'beans dep add --project <name> <from> <to>' creates cross-project edges. Ready query considers cross-project blockers.

snapshot.title: Phase 8: Polish
snapshot.body: Epic for search, stats, graph visualization, field filtering, labels, and README. See PLAN.md Phase 8.

snapshot.title: beans search — full-text search
snapshot.body: Full-text search across title, body, and labels. 'beans search <query>' returns matching beans. Use SQLite FTS5 or LIKE-based search.

snapshot.title: beans stats — aggregate counts
snapshot.body: Aggregate counts by status, type, assignee. 'beans stats' outputs a summary table. Supports --json for structured output.

snapshot.title: beans graph — dependency tree visualization
snapshot.body: Text-based dependency tree visualization. 'beans graph' renders the DAG as indented text or ASCII art. Shows blocking relationships and status.

snapshot.title: Field filtering with --fields
snapshot.body: '--fields id,title,status' flag to limit output columns. Works with list, ready, show, and search commands. Reduces noise for agents that only need specific fields.

snapshot.title: Labels — add, remove, filter
snapshot.body: 'beans label add <id> <label>', 'beans label remove <id> <label>'. Labels stored in a labels table (bean_id, label). Filter by label in list/ready with '--label <label>'. Labels are free-text strings.

snapshot.title: README — usage docs, examples, philosophy
snapshot.body: Write a comprehensive README.md with: project philosophy, installation (pip install magic-beans), quick start, CLI reference with examples, architecture overview, and contributing guide.

snapshot.title: Add --type flag to create and update commands
snapshot.body: Bean.type (task/bug/epic) can only be set via code, not via CLI. Add '--type' option to 'beans create' and 'beans update'. BeanUpdate already has type field, just needs CLI wiring.
